David Lee

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the email?

passage.text:
mica.agst@dmail.com
From:
Subject:
My thesis proposal
Dear Professor Carter,
I just want to express my appreciation for your thoughtful feedback on
my thesis proposal about renewable-energy adoption. Your notes on
tightening the methodology and adding comparative case studies were
very helpful and gave me a clearer path forward. I have already updated
my outine with those changes and plan to send the revised version of
the entire proposal next Monday.
I also really appreciate you making time during office hours to discuss
my questions with me and for sharing the relevant policy-analysis
resources-they are exactly what I need. Your support has made a real
difference in how I'm approaching this work, and I feel much more
confident about the next steps. I am sincerely grateful for all you have
done for me.
Best regards,
Micah Agostino

options.text: To confirm the deadline for an assignment
To ask for an extension on a project
To thank a faculty member for their help
To ask for comments on a paper

questions.prompt: Why does Angela recommend bringing a lot of water for the hike?

passage.text:
Hi everyone! Want to join me for a hike on Saturday at Green Valley with
the Student Outdoors Club?
What time?
Let's meet at the trailhead at 8:00 AM. Starting early will help us beat the
heat, which starts creeping in at 9:00 A.M. Or So.
What should we bring?
Well, for sure, bring PLENTY of water.
I'll bring extra water-good point about the heat! Do we need a permit?
I have heard that some hiking areas are now being protected.
Let me check on that, Tom. Parking is limited carpooling is best.
I can drive! I've got room for three.
Til take that offer, Milton.

options.text: The trail will eventually climb to an extremely high altitude.
The conditions along the trail will be exceptionally windy and dusty.
The water for sale along the trail is too expensive.
The temperature along the trail will start to climb after about nine a.m.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: What does the passage imply about homophily?

passage.text:
Social Networks and Influence

In today's digital age, social networks play a significant role in shaping opinions and behaviors. The influence of these networks is not merely about the number of connections one might have, but about the quality and nature of those interactions. Sociologists have long been interested in how individuals within a network can influence the group's overall dynamics. This concept is sometimes referred to as social capital.

The structure of a network can determine the flow of information. An individual positioned at the intersection of various groups, known as a bridge, often plays a crucial role in disseminating information. This person can introduce new ideas and perspectives to different parts of the network, thereby influencing opinions and possibly altering behaviors. Another factor is homophily, where individuals are more likely to form connections with others who are similar to themselves in terms of opinions, values, or social status. While homophily can reinforce existing beliefs, it can also create echo chambers, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives. The presence of influential individuals, often termed opinion leaders, can amplify or mitigate the effects of network structures by means of their social skills or charisma. Sociologists aim to uncover the nuanced ways social networks shape collective behavior.

options.text: It ensures the dissemination of diverse ideas.
It promotes similarity and continuity within groups.
It emphasizes opinion leaders over network structures
It makes it unnecessary to have bridges across networks.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: The word "nuanced" in the passage is closest in meaning to many

passage.text: In the United States, as in some other countries, video evidence has become integral to the criminal justice system as technological advances have made cameras nearly ubiquitous. Police officers often wear body cameras, security cameras monitor public areas, and nearly everyone carries a smartphone capable of recording high-quality videos. While such recordings are widely seen as reliable evidentiary tools, they have certain For example, videos are usually taken from a single vantage point, potentially giving viewers a misleading impression of events. A single camera angle might fail to capture what occurs just outside the frame, leading observers to misinterpret what they see. Additionally, video footage has no way of conveying interpersonal and emotional context: A person's actions might appear antagonistic even if the person was acting in self- Despite these limitations, legal experts emphasize that video evidence plays a significant role in courtrooms. Jurors (people chosen to consider evidence and decide a case) might dismiss eyewitness testimonies as unreliable or perceive oral accounts as inconsistent, while videos show events more directly. When multiple recordings are available from different angles, an even more reliable sense of an incident can emerge. Nevertheless, courts often remind jurors to evaluate video evidence

questions.prompt: What does the author point out when discussing "context"?

passage.text: In the United States, as in some other countries, video evidence has become integral to the criminal justice system as technological advances have made cameras nearly ubiquitous. Police officers often wear body cameras, security cameras monitor public areas, and nearly everyone carries a smartphone capable of recording high-quality videos. While such recordings are widely seen as reliable evidentiary tools, they have certain limitations. For example, videos are usually taken from a single vantage point, potentially giving viewers a misleading impression of events. A single camera angle might fail to capture what occurs just outside the frame, leading observers to misinterpret what they see. Additionally, video footage has no way of conveying interpersonal and emotional context: A person's actions might appear antagonistic even if the person was acting in self- defense. Despite these limitations, legal experts emphasize that video evidence plays a significant role in courtrooms. Jurors (people chosen to consider evidence and decide a case) might dismiss evewitness testimonies as unreliable or perceive oral accounts as inconsistent, while videos show events more directly. When multiple recordings are available from different angles, an even more reliable sense of an incident can emerge. Nevertheless, courts often remind jurors to evaluate video evidence cautiously.

options.text: The setting in which a video is viewed may affect the way viewers perceive the recorded events. People may draw incorrect conclusions about others' motivations from what they observe in a video. Video recordings can be used either to defend an individual's actions or to depict the individual as an aggressor.
The reliability of video recordings of events should be compared to the reliability of oral accounts of those events.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: The passage indicates that "legal experts" hold which of the following views? Jurors are likely to accept videos as objective depictions of events whereas they view witness testimony with skepticism.

passage.text:
In the United States, as in some other countries, video evidence has become integral to the criminal justice system as technological advances have made cameras nearly ubiquitous. Police officers often wear body cameras, security cameras monitor public areas, and nearly everyone carries a smartphone capable of recording high-quality videos. While such recordings are widely seen as reliable evidentiary tools, they have certain limitations.

For example, videos are usually taken from a single vantage point, potentially giving viewers a misleading impression of events. A single camera angle might fail to capture what occurs just outside the frame, leading observers to misinterpret what they see. Additionally, video footage has no way of conveying interpersonal and emotional context: A person's actions might appear antagonistic even if the person was acting in self- defense.

Despite these limitations, legal experts emphasize that video evidence plays a significant role in courtrooms. Jurors (people chosen to consider evidence and decide a case) might dismiss eyewitness testimonies as unreliable or perceive oral accounts as inconsistent, while videos show events more directly. When multiple recordings are available from different angles, an even more reliable sense of an incident can emerge. Nevertheless, courts often remind jurors to evaluate video evidence

cautiously.

options.text: Recent improvements in technology have made it so easy to display video evidence to jurors that other kinds of evidence may be neglected.
Video recordings should not be used as evidence in courtrooms unless multiple recordings taken from different vantage points can be shown.
Courts should not undermine jurors abilities by overemphasizing the pe misinterpreting videos.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: The word "cautiously" in the passage is closest in meaning to reluctantly carefully decisively

passage.text: In the United States, as in some other countries, video evidence has become integral to the criminal justice system as technological advances have made cameras nearly ubiquitous. Police officers often wear body cameras, security cameras monitor public areas, and nearly everyone carries a smartphone capable of recording high-quality videos. While such recordings are widely seen as reliable evidentiary tools, they have certain limitations. For example, videos are usually taken from a single vantage point, potentially giving viewers a misleading impression of events. A single camera angle might fail to capture what occurs just outside the frame, leading observers to misinterpret what they see. Additionally, video footage has no way of conveying interpersonal and emotional context: A person's actions might appear antagonistic even if the person was acting in self- defense. Despite these limitations, legal experts emphasize that video evidence plays a significant role in courtrooms. Jurors (people chosen to consider evidence and decide a case) might dismiss eyewitness testimonies as unreliable or perceive oral accounts as inconsistent, while videos show events more directly. When multiple recordings are available from different angles, an even more reliable sense of an incident can emerge. Nevertheless, courts often remind jurors to evaluate video evidence cautiously.
